the-content-studio-mindset.instruction: Write a prompt that generates 5 content ideas for your niche. Include target audience, content goals, and preferred formats.
the-content-studio-mindset.placeholder: I create content about... My audience is...
the-content-studio-mindset.hint: I create content about personal finance for freelancers. My audience is solo freelancers earning $50-150K who are good at their craft but bad with money. Content goals: build trust, drive email signups. Preferred formats: Twitter threads, short blog posts, email newsletters. Generate 5 content ideas that address their biggest money anxiety: irregular income. For each: title, format, hook, and which goal it serves.
the-content-studio-mindset.goodExample: I create content about personal finance for freelancers earning $50-150K who are great at their craft but terrible with money. Content goals: (1) build trust as a non-judgmental expert, (2) drive email signups for my free budget template. Formats I use: Twitter threads, 800-word blog posts, weekly email newsletter. Generate 5 content ideas addressing irregular income anxiety. For each: title, best format, opening hook, which goal it serves (trust vs signup), and estimated creation time with AI.
the-content-studio-mindset.successMessage: Strategy before creation. You just built a content brief worth $200.

the-content-studio-mindset.instruction:
Analyze this content strategy and identify the fatal flaw:

A solopreneur posts 3 times/day on Twitter, 5 times/week on LinkedIn, 2 blog posts/week, and a weekly newsletter. They have no team and use AI for everything.
the-content-studio-mindset.hint: The fatal flaw is volume over value. At this pace, even with AI, they can't maintain quality. They're also spreading across too many platforms without mastering one. The result: generic content everywhere, no deep audience on any platform, burnout in 6 weeks.
the-content-studio-mindset.badExample: They should just hire someone to help.
the-content-studio-mindset.goodExample: Fatal flaw: quantity without quality or focus. At 21+ pieces per week with no team, every piece gets minimal thought — AI generates it, they barely review it, nobody engages. Three problems: (1) Platform dilution — they're mediocre everywhere instead of excellent somewhere. (2) No feedback loop — they're publishing too fast to analyze what works. (3) Burnout trajectory — this pace is unsustainable even WITH AI because curation, scheduling, and engagement still take hours. Better strategy: Pick ONE platform. Post 4x/week with real thought. Spend saved time engaging with responses and analyzing metrics. Once one platform hits 5K engaged followers, add a second.
the-content-studio-mindset.successMessage: More content is not better content. Strategy beats volume every time.

short-form-mastery.instruction: Write a prompt that generates a viral-style social media post. Specify platform, hook style, length, and CTA.
short-form-mastery.hint: Write a Twitter/X thread (5 tweets) about why most freelancers fail at pricing. Hook: a specific money amount that shocks ('I charged $500 for a project that took 60 hours. That's $8.33/hour.'). Each tweet: one lesson learned. Last tweet: CTA to my free pricing guide. Tone: honest, slightly self-deprecating, zero corporate speak. No hashtags.
short-form-mastery.badExample: Write a social media post about pricing.
short-form-mastery.goodExample: Write a Twitter/X thread (5 tweets, each under 280 characters) about why most freelancers underprice their work. Hook tweet: start with a specific embarrassing number ('I once charged $500 for a project that took 60 hours. That's $8.33/hour. Here's what I learned.'). Tweets 2-4: one pricing mistake each with the fix. Tweet 5: 'I built a free pricing calculator so you don't make these mistakes. Link in bio.' Tone: honest, self-deprecating, conversational. No hashtags, no emojis in every tweet, no 'thread' announcement.
short-form-mastery.successMessage: Short-form is the hardest format. You nailed the brief.

short-form-mastery.instruction:
This LinkedIn post is boring corporate fluff. Rewrite it to actually get engagement:

'Excited to share that our team has been leveraging AI to drive innovative solutions across our organization. The future of work is here, and we're embracing it! #AI #Innovation #FutureOfWork'
short-form-mastery.placeholder: Rewritten version that humans would actually engage with...
short-form-mastery.hint: Replace buzzwords with a specific story. Instead of 'leveraging AI,' share one concrete example. Instead of 'innovative solutions,' share one specific result with a number. Open with a hook that creates curiosity, not an announcement.
short-form-mastery.badExample: We're excited about AI and it's making our work better! #AI
short-form-mastery.goodExample: Last month I spent 6 hours writing one proposal. This week I wrote 3 proposals in 2 hours. Same quality — my clients couldn't tell the difference. Here's exactly what changed: I built a 3-step AI workflow that handles the repetitive parts (company research, competitive analysis, formatting) while I focus on the strategy and personal touches that actually win deals. The result: 3x more proposals out the door, and our close rate went UP because I spend more time on strategy. I'll share the exact workflow in the comments if anyone wants it.
short-form-mastery.successMessage: Specific stories with real numbers beat corporate buzzwords every time. That's what gets engagement.

long-form-writing.instruction: Write a prompt for a detailed blog post outline. Include topic, target keyword, audience level, sections, and unique angle.
long-form-writing.hint: Write a blog post outline for 'How to Price Freelance Work in 2025.' Target keyword: freelance pricing guide. Audience: intermediate freelancers (1-3 years) who are past $25/hr but stuck under $100/hr. Unique angle: most pricing guides say 'charge what you're worth' without explaining HOW. This one gives 3 specific frameworks. Sections: hook (pricing horror story), the 3 frameworks with examples, common mistakes, pricing calculator CTA. Length target: 2,000 words.
long-form-writing.badExample: Write an outline for a blog about freelancing.
long-form-writing.goodExample: Write a blog outline: 'How to Price Freelance Work in 2025.' Target keyword: freelance pricing guide (2,400 monthly searches). Audience: freelancers with 1-3 years experience, earning $40-80/hr, wanting to break $100/hr. Unique angle: most pricing guides give mindset advice ('charge what you're worth'). This one gives 3 concrete frameworks with fill-in-the-blank calculators. Sections: (1) Hook — a pricing horror story with real numbers. (2) Framework 1: Cost-plus (for beginners). (3) Framework 2: Value-based (for intermediates). (4) Framework 3: Productized pricing (for scaling). (5) The 5 pricing mistakes that keep you under $100/hr. (6) CTA: free pricing calculator. Length: 2,000 words. Each section: suggested word count + key point to make.
long-form-writing.successMessage: Outlines are the skeleton. AI fills the flesh. A great outline makes the writing almost automatic.

long-form-writing.instruction: A content creator says: 'Every blog post I generate with AI sounds the same — generic, listicle-style, no personality. I've tried different topics but the voice is always flat.' Diagnose the problem and give 3 specific fixes.
long-form-writing.hint: The problem is almost certainly in the prompts, not the AI. They're probably giving the same generic instructions every time without specifying voice, angle, or anti-patterns. The fixes involve voice definition, writing samples, and specific style constraints.
long-form-writing.badExample: Use a different AI model with more personality.
long-form-writing.goodExample: Diagnosis: The AI isn't the problem — their prompts are. Generic prompts produce generic output. They're probably saying 'write a blog post about X' without specifying voice, angle, or what NOT to do. Fix 1: VOICE INJECTION — paste 300 words of their own writing and say 'Match this voice: direct, slightly irreverent, uses short sentences and rhetorical questions.' Fix 2: ANTI-PATTERN LIST — 'Don't use: firstly/secondly/in conclusion, don't start paragraphs with transition words, don't use passive voice.' This eliminates the generic AI patterns. Fix 3: ANGLE CONSTRAINT — 'The contrarian take on this topic is [X]. Build the piece around why the common advice is wrong.' Contrarian angles force original thinking instead of summarizing consensus.
long-form-writing.successMessage: AI sounds generic because generic instructions produce generic output. Voice, anti-patterns, and angles fix everything.

content-strategy-with-ai.instruction: Create a 1-week content calendar. Include platform, content type, topic, goal (awareness/engagement/conversion), and posting time.
content-strategy-with-ai.hint: Monday 9am: LinkedIn post — industry insight (awareness). Tuesday 12pm: Twitter thread — how-to tutorial (engagement). Wednesday 9am: Blog post — SEO-targeted article (awareness + conversion via email signup). Thursday 12pm: LinkedIn carousel — data/stats (engagement). Friday 10am: Newsletter — weekly recap + exclusive tip (conversion + retention).
content-strategy-with-ai.badExample: Monday: post something. Tuesday: post something. Wednesday: post something.
content-strategy-with-ai.goodExample: Monday 9am — LinkedIn text post: 'One counterintuitive thing I learned about [topic] this week.' Goal: awareness + engagement. Why Monday: professionals are planning their week, receptive to insights. Tuesday 12pm — Twitter thread (5 tweets): Step-by-step tutorial on a specific skill. Goal: engagement + followers. Why noon: lunch break scrolling. Wednesday 8am — Blog post (1,500 words): SEO-targeted how-to guide with email signup CTA for bonus template. Goal: organic traffic + conversion. Thursday 1pm — LinkedIn carousel (7 slides): Data visualization or framework. Goal: saves + shares (engagement). Friday 10am — Email newsletter: Week's best insight + one exclusive tip not posted publicly. Goal: retention + conversion. Buffer: Saturday batch-create next week's content using AI.
content-strategy-with-ai.successMessage: A content calendar turns random posting into a strategic machine.

content-strategy-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt that analyzes your last 10 social media posts to identify patterns: what worked, what didn't, and what to do more of.
content-strategy-with-ai.placeholder: Analyze these 10 posts and find patterns...
content-strategy-with-ai.hint: Here are my last 10 LinkedIn posts with their engagement metrics (impressions, likes, comments, shares). For each: identify the hook type, content format, topic category, and posting time. Then: (1) Which 2 posts performed best and what do they have in common? (2) Which 2 performed worst and what pattern explains it? (3) What should my next 5 posts look like based on this data?
content-strategy-with-ai.badExample: Which of my posts did best?
content-strategy-with-ai.goodExample: Analyze my last 10 LinkedIn posts (pasted below with metrics). For each post, tag: hook type (question/stat/story/contrarian), format (text/carousel/video), topic bucket, day+time posted, and engagement score (likes + 2x comments + 3x shares). Then: (1) Top 2 posts — what do they share? Hook type? Format? Topic? Time? (2) Bottom 2 — what went wrong? (3) Pattern hypothesis: 'Posts about [X] using [hook type] on [day] get 3x more engagement.' (4) Prescriptive: my next 5 post topics, formats, and hooks based on this analysis. Be specific — don't say 'post more engaging content.'
content-strategy-with-ai.successMessage: Data-driven content strategy beats guessing. Let your own metrics tell you what works.

email-sequences-that-convert.instruction: Write a prompt for a 3-email welcome sequence. Define brand voice, value proposition, and end goal.
email-sequences-that-convert.placeholder: Create a welcome email sequence for...
email-sequences-that-convert.hint: Create a 3-email welcome sequence for my freelance pricing newsletter. Brand voice: direct, no-BS, like a friend who's 3 years ahead of you. Value prop: practical pricing strategies, not motivational fluff. End goal: purchase my $97 pricing course. Email 1 (immediate): deliver the lead magnet + one quick win. Email 2 (day 2): share my biggest pricing mistake with a lesson. Email 3 (day 4): introduce the course as the natural next step.
email-sequences-that-convert.badExample: Write 3 welcome emails for my newsletter.
email-sequences-that-convert.goodExample: Create a 3-email welcome sequence for my freelance pricing newsletter (5,000 subscribers, 45% open rate). Brand voice: direct, slightly blunt, like a friend who made the mistakes so you don't have to. Never condescending. End goal: purchase my $97 Pricing Mastery course (current conversion: 2%, target: 4%). Email 1 (sent immediately): Subject line with curiosity gap. Deliver the free pricing calculator they signed up for. Include ONE quick win they can implement today. Email 2 (day 2): Subject line with a specific number. Share my story of charging $500 for a 60-hour project. Lesson + framework. Email 3 (day 5): Soft pitch for the course. Frame as 'if you want all 7 frameworks in one place.' Include a testimonial. NO scarcity tactics — just honest value.
email-sequences-that-convert.successMessage: Email sequences are the highest-ROI content. This one runs on autopilot.

email-sequences-that-convert.instruction:
This email subject line has a 12% open rate (industry average is 21%). Rewrite it into 5 alternatives that should perform better, and explain why each is an improvement:

'Our Monthly Newsletter - March Edition'
email-sequences-that-convert.hint: The original fails because it has no curiosity gap, no benefit, and sounds like homework. Better subject lines promise a specific benefit, create curiosity, or use personalization. They should make the reader think 'I need to open this.'
email-sequences-that-convert.badExample: March Newsletter Update - Don't Miss This!
email-sequences-that-convert.goodExample: 5 rewrites: (1) 'The pricing mistake that cost me $12,000' — better because: specific dollar amount creates curiosity + implies a lesson to avoid. (2) 'You're probably charging 40% too little (here's the math)' — better because: 'you' is personal, specific percentage is concrete, 'here's the math' promises actionable content. (3) '3 clients said no last month. Here's what I changed.' — better because: vulnerability builds trust, implies a transformation story. (4) 'Delete this if you're happy with your rates' — better because: reverse psychology + self-selection. (5) 'Quick question about your pricing' — better because: conversational, short, feels 1-to-1 instead of 1-to-many. The original fails because 'Monthly Newsletter - March Edition' has zero value proposition and sounds like filing, not reading.
email-sequences-that-convert.successMessage: Subject lines are the most important 10 words you write. No open means no conversion, no matter how good the content is.

seo-content-optimization.instruction: This paragraph is keyword-stuffed. Rewrite to be SEO-friendly but human-readable: 'AI tools for business are the best AI tools. Our AI tools help businesses use AI tools to improve business with AI tools for business productivity.'
seo-content-optimization.hint: Keep the core keyword ('AI tools for business') once or twice, but vary the language. Use synonyms, related terms, and natural phrasing. Focus on what the reader actually learns.
seo-content-optimization.badExample: AI tools for business are the best AI tools. Our AI tools help businesses use AI tools.
seo-content-optimization.goodExample: The right AI tools can transform how your team works — automating repetitive tasks, surfacing insights from data, and freeing up hours for strategic thinking. Whether you're streamlining customer support with chatbots or using AI to draft proposals in minutes instead of hours, these tools pay for themselves within weeks.
seo-content-optimization.successMessage: SEO-friendly AND human-readable. Google rewards content written for people, not algorithms.

seo-content-optimization.instruction: Explain the difference between writing for SEO in 2020 vs. 2025+. List 3 old tactics that now hurt rankings and 3 modern approaches that work.
seo-content-optimization.placeholder: Old tactic 1: ... Why it hurts now: ... Modern approach: ...
seo-content-optimization.hint: Old: keyword stuffing, thin 500-word articles targeting exact-match keywords, building backlinks with guest post farms. Modern: comprehensive content that answers the full user intent, E-E-A-T signals (experience, expertise, authority, trust), and writing for featured snippets and AI overviews.
seo-content-optimization.badExample: SEO is all about keywords. Put them everywhere.
seo-content-optimization.goodExample: 3 old tactics that now hurt: (1) Keyword stuffing — repeating 'best AI tools' 15 times in 500 words. Google's BERT and helpful content updates now penalize this. It screams 'written for bots.' (2) Thin content — 500-word articles targeting one keyword each. Google now rewards comprehensive coverage. A 2,500-word piece that answers every related question outranks ten thin ones. (3) Exact-match domains/anchors — buying 'best-ai-tools.com' or building links with exact-match text. Google devalues manipulative patterns. 3 modern approaches: (1) Search intent matching — understand WHY someone searches this term (buying? learning? comparing?) and match the format to the intent. (2) E-E-A-T signals — demonstrate Experience (personal stories), Expertise (credentials), Authority (citations), Trust (transparency about limitations). (3) AI Overview optimization — structure content with clear Q&A formatting that Google can pull into AI-generated summaries at the top of search results.
seo-content-optimization.successMessage: SEO evolved from gaming algorithms to genuinely serving readers. The best SEO strategy is the best content strategy.

repurposing-content.instruction: Take a blog post topic and write a prompt to repurpose it into 3 formats: Twitter thread, LinkedIn post, and email newsletter intro.
repurposing-content.hint: Repurpose my 2,000-word blog post about freelance pricing (key points: 3 frameworks, biggest mistakes, calculator tool). Create: (1) Twitter thread — 6 tweets, lead with the most surprising stat, one framework per tweet, CTA to full post. (2) LinkedIn post — personal story angle, 150 words, conversation-starting question at end. (3) Email newsletter intro — 3 sentences teasing the post, one exclusive insight not in the blog, link CTA.
repurposing-content.badExample: Turn my blog post into social media posts.
repurposing-content.goodExample: Repurpose my blog post '3 Freelance Pricing Frameworks That Doubled My Income' (key points: cost-plus for beginners, value-based for intermediates, productized for scaling; main stat: went from $45/hr to $150/hr in 18 months). Create platform-native versions: (1) Twitter thread (6 tweets): Hook with the $45-to-$150 transformation, one tweet per framework with a mini-example, final tweet links to the full post with the calculator. No hashtags. (2) LinkedIn post (200 words): Start with 'I was terrified to raise my rates.' Tell the emotional story, share one framework, end with a question: 'What's your biggest pricing fear?' (3) Newsletter intro (3 sentences): Tease with 'The framework that finally made pricing feel simple,' mention an exclusive fourth strategy only in the email, link to blog.
repurposing-content.successMessage: One idea, three platforms. 3x output without 3x work. Each version is native to its platform.

repurposing-content.instruction: A creator says: 'I repurpose my content across platforms but engagement keeps dropping. My LinkedIn post is literally the same as my tweet which is the same as my newsletter.' Diagnose why this fails and prescribe the fix.
repurposing-content.placeholder: This fails because... The fix is...
repurposing-content.hint: Copy-pasting the same content across platforms fails because each platform has different audiences, formats, expectations, and algorithms. A LinkedIn post reads differently than a tweet. People who follow you on multiple platforms see the same thing and stop engaging.
repurposing-content.badExample: Post at different times so people don't see duplicates.
repurposing-content.goodExample: Diagnosis: Copy-paste repurposing is the content equivalent of wearing a swimsuit to a job interview — same body, wrong context. Three reasons it kills engagement: (1) Each platform rewards different formats — Twitter rewards punchy hooks and threads, LinkedIn rewards stories and professional takes, newsletters reward depth and exclusivity. (2) Cross-platform followers see the same thing 3 times and tune out. (3) Algorithms detect low-engagement content and reduce reach further. Fix: Same IDEA, different EXECUTION. Blog post about pricing? Twitter: contrarian hot take ('Hourly billing is a scam — here's why'). LinkedIn: personal story ('The day I 3x'd my rate and the client said yes immediately'). Newsletter: exclusive deep-dive with a framework they can't get elsewhere. Each version adds unique value to its platform.
repurposing-content.successMessage: Repurposing means adapting the idea to the platform, not copying the text. Platform-native content wins.

brand-voice-consistency.instruction: Define your brand voice in a format AI can use: 3 tone adjectives, 3 words you always use, 3 you never use, and an example paragraph.
brand-voice-consistency.hint: Tone: direct, warm, slightly irreverent. Always use: 'here's the thing,' specific numbers, rhetorical questions. Never use: 'innovative,' 'synergy,' 'unlock your potential.' Example: 'Here's the thing about pricing: most freelancers spend 3 hours agonizing over a number they could calculate in 10 minutes. Want to know the formula? It's embarrassingly simple.'
brand-voice-consistency.badExample: My voice is professional and friendly.
brand-voice-consistency.goodExample: BRAND VOICE SPEC: Tone: direct (no hedging), warm (never cold or clinical), slightly irreverent (breaks rules on purpose). Always use: 'Here's the thing' (signature opener), specific numbers over vague claims ('saved 3 hours' not 'saved time'), rhetorical questions to create engagement. Never use: 'innovative,' 'leverage,' 'unlock your potential,' 'game-changing,' 'deep dive.' These are corporate filler. Sentence structure: short sentences. Occasionally a long one for emphasis. Then short again. Example paragraph: 'Here's the thing about content calendars: most creators spend more time planning content than creating it. I used to spend 4 hours every Sunday mapping out my week. Now? 20 minutes. The secret isn't a better template — it's deciding what you WON'T post.'
brand-voice-consistency.successMessage: A documented brand voice means every AI-generated piece sounds like YOU. This is your most valuable content asset.

brand-voice-consistency.instruction:
Analyze these two paragraphs written by AI and determine which one has a stronger, more distinct voice. Explain what makes the difference.

Paragraph A: 'In today's fast-paced digital landscape, content creation has become increasingly important for businesses looking to establish their online presence and engage with their target audience effectively.'

Paragraph B: 'Nobody reads your content. Sorry. The average blog post gets 0 shares. Zero. So why do 7 million blog posts get published every day? Because most people confuse publishing with marketing.'
brand-voice-consistency.placeholder: Paragraph [A/B] has a stronger voice because...
brand-voice-consistency.hint: Paragraph B is dramatically stronger. It has a point of view, uses short punchy sentences, leads with a provocative claim, backs it with a specific stat, and has a clear personality. Paragraph A is interchangeable with a million other AI-generated intros — no opinion, no personality, no specificity.
brand-voice-consistency.badExample: Both are fine, just different styles.
brand-voice-consistency.goodExample: Paragraph B wins by a mile. Here's why: (1) OPINION — 'Nobody reads your content' is a provocative claim. Paragraph A has no opinion at all — it states the obvious. (2) SPECIFICITY — '0 shares' and '7 million blog posts' are concrete. Paragraph A uses hollow phrases: 'fast-paced,' 'increasingly important,' 'target audience.' (3) RHYTHM — B alternates between short and long sentences. 'Sorry.' is one word. Then a stat. Then a question. A reads like one long corporate exhale. (4) TENSION — B creates cognitive dissonance: if no one reads content, why does everyone publish it? That tension makes you keep reading. A creates zero tension because it says nothing surprising. The core lesson: voice = opinion + specificity + rhythm. Remove any one and you sound like every other AI-generated paragraph.
brand-voice-consistency.successMessage: Voice is not about vocabulary — it's about having an opinion, being specific, and varying your rhythm. That's what AI can't do by default.

content-workflows-and-batching.instruction: Design a content batching workflow: produce 2 weeks of content in one session. List each step from ideation to scheduling.
content-workflows-and-batching.hint: Step 1: Brainstorm 14 content topics using AI (30 min). Step 2: Outline all 14 pieces (45 min). Step 3: Generate first drafts with AI (60 min). Step 4: Edit and add personal voice (90 min). Step 5: Create visuals/graphics (30 min). Step 6: Schedule all posts (15 min). Total: ~4.5 hours for 2 weeks of content.
content-workflows-and-batching.badExample: Write a bunch of content and schedule it.
content-workflows-and-batching.goodExample: 2-Week Content Batch — Single 5-Hour Session. STEP 1 (30 min): Ideation sprint — prompt AI with my top 5 performing topics + audience questions. Generate 20 ideas, select 14. STEP 2 (30 min): Outline all 14 — for each, write the hook, 3 key points, and CTA. AI generates, I approve/modify. STEP 3 (60 min): First drafts — AI writes all 14 drafts from outlines. I review in batches of 3-4. STEP 4 (90 min): Voice pass — this is where I earn my keep. Add personal stories, remove AI-isms ('in today's landscape'), sharpen hooks. STEP 5 (30 min): Visuals — generate social graphics using Canva templates. Batch export. STEP 6 (15 min): Schedule — load into Buffer/Hootsuite. Set times based on platform analytics. STEP 7 (15 min): Newsletter compile — pull best 3 pieces into weekly newsletter template. Total: 4.5 hours for 14 pieces. Without batching, this takes 14+ hours over 2 weeks.
content-workflows-and-batching.successMessage: Batching is the secret weapon of every prolific creator. 4.5 hours beats 14 hours of daily content scrambling.

content-workflows-and-batching.instruction: Write a prompt that generates a month's worth of content ideas organized by theme week. Include content pillars, variety of formats, and strategic intent behind each week.
content-workflows-and-batching.placeholder: Generate a month of themed content weeks...
content-workflows-and-batching.hint: I have 4 content pillars: (1) pricing strategies, (2) client management, (3) productivity tools, (4) mindset/motivation. Generate a 4-week content plan where each week focuses on one pillar. For each week: 3 social posts (varied formats), 1 blog post, 1 newsletter angle. Strategic intent: week 1 = awareness (reach new people), week 2 = engagement (deepen relationship), week 3 = authority (establish expertise), week 4 = conversion (drive course sales).
content-workflows-and-batching.badExample: Give me content ideas for next month.
content-workflows-and-batching.goodExample: Generate a 4-week content plan for my freelance business audience. Content pillars: (1) pricing strategies, (2) client management, (3) productivity tools, (4) business mindset. Each week focuses on ONE pillar. Per week: 3 social posts (mix of thread, carousel, text), 1 blog post (SEO-targeted), 1 newsletter topic. Strategic arc: Week 1 (Pricing) = awareness — controversial takes that get shared. Week 2 (Clients) = engagement — 'tell me your story' posts that drive comments. Week 3 (Tools) = authority — detailed tutorials that prove expertise. Week 4 (Mindset) = conversion — transformation stories leading to course CTA. For each piece: one-line description, format, platform, and estimated AI vs. manual effort ratio.
content-workflows-and-batching.successMessage: A themed month has strategic intent behind every post. This is how brands build, not just broadcast.

launching-your-content-engine.instruction: Write your Content Engine spec: what you publish, where, how often, what AI handles vs what's manual. Include your operational blueprint for sustainability.
launching-your-content-engine.hint: Platform: LinkedIn (primary), Newsletter (secondary). Frequency: 3 LinkedIn posts/week, 1 newsletter/week. AI handles: first drafts, research, repurposing, SEO optimization. Manual: voice editing, personal stories, strategic decisions, community engagement. Sustainability: 4-hour Sunday batch session, 15 min/day for engagement. Review metrics monthly.
launching-your-content-engine.badExample: I'll post content on social media using AI.
launching-your-content-engine.goodExample: CONTENT ENGINE SPEC — Primary platform: LinkedIn (90% of effort). Secondary: weekly email newsletter. Frequency: 3 LinkedIn posts/week (Mon/Wed/Fri 9am ET), 1 newsletter every Thursday. AI HANDLES: topic ideation from trending conversations, first drafts from my outlines, SEO keyword research for blog posts, repurposing blog content into social formats, headline A/B test variants. I HANDLE: final voice editing (10-15 min per piece), injecting personal stories and client examples, replying to every comment (15 min/day), monthly strategy review. SUSTAINABILITY SYSTEM: Sunday 3-4pm — batch create next week's content. Wednesday — review analytics, adjust Friday's post if needed. Monthly — audit what worked, update content pillars, refine AI prompts based on top performers. KILL SWITCH: if I miss a Sunday batch, I use AI to generate a 'quick take' post instead of going silent. Consistency beats perfection.
launching-your-content-engine.successMessage: Most creators never write this down. That's why they burn out. You won't. A documented engine runs whether you feel inspired or not.

launching-your-content-engine.instruction: Write a prompt that creates a 90-day content launch plan for someone starting from zero followers. Include milestone targets and what to adjust at each checkpoint.
launching-your-content-engine.hint: Create a 90-day plan for launching a content presence from zero. Platform: LinkedIn. Niche: AI tools for small business. Include: Days 1-30 milestones (foundation), Days 31-60 milestones (growth), Days 61-90 milestones (monetization). At each 30-day checkpoint: what metrics to review, what to double down on, what to cut. Realistic follower targets for a B2B niche.
launching-your-content-engine.badExample: How do I grow my social media following?
launching-your-content-engine.goodExample: Create a 90-day content launch plan for someone starting from 0 followers on LinkedIn. Niche: AI tools for small business owners. Days 1-30 (Foundation): posting frequency, content types to test, engagement strategy, target: 200 followers and 3 posts that each get 20+ likes. Days 31-60 (Growth): double down on winning formats, start collaborating, target: 750 followers, 1 post that gets 100+ likes. Days 61-90 (Monetization): introduce lead magnet, start email list, first soft pitch, target: 1,500 followers, 200 email subscribers. At each 30-day checkpoint: which metrics to review, what to double down on, what to drop, and how to adjust the strategy. Be realistic — no 'go viral' fantasies. Include specific daily time commitment.
launching-your-content-engine.successMessage: A 90-day plan with checkpoints prevents the two failure modes: quitting too early or grinding on what doesn't work.
